learning_objectives: Understand the role of QA within the game development lifecycle
Distinguish between different types of game testing and when each applies
Recognize how QA processes in games differ from traditional software QA
Identify career paths and specializations within game QA
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 1-3
ISTQB Foundation Level Syllabus - Testing Fundamentals
Gamasutra: The Role of QA in Modern Game Development
GDC Vault: Building a QA Department from Scratch

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality assurance in games is not just about finding bugs. It is about ensuring the player experience meets design intent across thousands of hardware configurations, input methods, and play styles. A single crash during a boss fight can cost you a player forever and a negative review that discourages hundreds more. Game QA is fundamentally different from traditional software QA because games are creative products with subjective quality dimensions: is the difficulty curve fair? Does the control scheme feel responsive? Is the visual experience immersive? These questions cannot be answered by automated test scripts alone -- they require human testers with domain expertise and design sensibility. The game QA team is the last line of defense between the development team and the player. When QA fails, the player suffers. Today we examine the QA discipline from its organizational structure (embedded QA, centralized QA, outsourced QA) through its taxonomy of testing types (functional, compliance, compatibility, localization, performance, accessibility, playtesting) to its economic reality: bugs found in production cost 10 to 100 times more to fix than bugs found during development, because production fixes require emergency patches, certification resubmission, and reputation repair. Understanding this cost multiplier is the single most important motivation for investing in QA.
key_concepts: QA vs testing: QA is a process discipline that prevents defects; testing is an activity that detects defects already present
Game QA taxonomy: functional, regression, compatibility, performance, localization, compliance/certification, accessibility, playtesting
QA team structures: embedded (QA assigned to feature teams), centralized (dedicated QA department), outsourced (third-party test labs)
QA in agile game development: sprint-embedded testing, continuous integration testing, definition of done including QA sign-off
Cost of defects by phase: requirements (1x), development (5x), testing (10x), production (50-100x) -- earlier is cheaper
Severity and priority: severity measures impact on the player, priority measures urgency of the fix; they are independent axes
QA career paths: tester, senior tester, test lead, QA manager, QA director, and specialist tracks (automation, performance, localization)
The QA mindset: systematic thinking, attention to edge cases, adversarial interaction (what input would break this?), empathy for the player
Shift-left testing: moving testing activities earlier in development through design reviews, code reviews, and unit tests
QA metrics: defect discovery rate, defect fix rate, test case pass rate, test coverage percentage, defect escape rate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through a real shipped game's public bug database (e.g., a Bethesda or CD Projekt Red title) to analyze the severity distribution of reported bugs, examine how critical vs minor bugs were triaged, discuss which bugs should have been caught earlier, and identify patterns suggesting systemic QA process gaps (e.g., many compatibility bugs suggesting insufficient hardware testing).
setup_requirements: Jira or Azure DevOps account configured with a game QA workflow
Sample game project with intentional defects for testing exercises
Screen recording software (OBS) for bug reproduction documentation
Public bug database or post-mortem for analysis

review.value: Critical: crash, data loss, blocks progression. Major: significant feature broken but workaround exists. Minor: cosmetic issue, minor inconvenience. Trivial: typo, alignment off by pixels. Severity determines whether a build ships.

Moving testing earlier in the development cycle: design reviews catch requirement bugs, code reviews catch logic bugs, unit tests catch function bugs. Each stage earlier saves exponentially more time and money than finding the same bug later.

The percentage of bugs that reach players despite QA testing. Calculated as (production bugs) / (production bugs + bugs found in QA). A high escape rate indicates gaps in test coverage or insufficient testing time.

The criteria a feature must meet before it is considered complete. In game development, this should include: functional testing passed, no known Critical/Major bugs, performance within budget, and accessibility checklist reviewed.

learning_objectives: Write comprehensive test plans for game features and full releases
Design test cases with clear preconditions, steps, expected results, and coverage tracking
Organize test suites by feature area, risk level, and testing phase
Establish entry and exit criteria for each testing phase
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 5-7
IEEE 829 Test Documentation Standard (overview)
Test Planning Best Practices for Game Development

lecture_notes.introduction: A test plan is a contract between QA and development that defines what will be tested, how it will be tested, what resources are needed, and what criteria determine pass or fail. Without a test plan, testing is ad hoc exploration that provides no measurable coverage guarantees. A good test plan for a game feature covers the happy path (normal expected usage), the sad path (error conditions and invalid input), boundary conditions (edge values where behavior changes), and combinatorial cases (interactions between systems). It also specifies the test environment (hardware, OS, build version), the entry criteria (what must be true before testing begins, e.g., feature code complete and build compiles), and exit criteria (what must be true for testing to be considered complete, e.g., all Priority 1 and 2 test cases pass, no Critical bugs open). Today we write a complete test plan for a game's inventory system, creating test cases that cover item pickup, storage, stacking, dropping, using, equipping, unequipping, selling, trading, sorting, filtering, and edge cases like inventory full, item type conflicts, and concurrent modification. We organize test cases into suites by feature area and priority, trace them to design requirements for coverage analysis, and establish a realistic testing schedule.
key_concepts: Test plan components: scope, approach, resources, schedule, entry criteria, exit criteria, risk assessment, deliverables
Test case anatomy: ID, title, priority, preconditions, step-by-step instructions, expected result, actual result, pass/fail status
Equivalence partitioning: dividing input into classes where all values behave identically, testing one representative per class
Boundary value analysis: testing at the exact edges of valid ranges and one step beyond (0, 1, max-1, max, max+1)
Test case priority levels: P0 (blocks release), P1 (must test every build), P2 (test every milestone), P3 (test if time permits)
Test suite organization: by feature area (inventory, combat, UI), by test type (smoke, regression, full), by priority tier
Traceability matrix: mapping test cases to design requirements to ensure every requirement has at least one test
Risk-based testing: allocating more test cases and time to high-risk features (new code, complex interactions, player-facing)
Entry criteria: the conditions that must be met before testing begins (build compiles, smoke test passes, test environment ready)
Exit criteria: the conditions that must be met to consider testing complete (all P0/P1 cases pass, no Critical bugs, coverage > 80%)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing a test plan for a game inventory system: defining scope (all inventory operations), creating 20 test cases covering normal flow, edge cases, and error conditions with equivalence partitioning and boundary analysis. Building a traceability matrix mapping test cases to design requirements. Setting entry criteria (build with inventory feature complete, no compilation errors) and exit criteria (all P0/P1 pass, no Critical/Major open, 90% P2 pass rate).
setup_requirements: Test case management tool (TestRail, spreadsheet template)
Sample game design document for inventory system
Jira project configured for the sample game
Traceability matrix template

review.value: Dividing input values into groups that should produce the same behavior. For inventory quantity: 0 (empty), 1 (minimum), 2-98 (normal), 99 (max), 100+ (overflow). Testing one value from each partition provides efficient coverage without testing every number.

Testing at the exact boundaries where behavior changes. For a 99-slot inventory: test at 0, 1, 98, 99, and 100 items. Most bugs cluster at boundaries because code transitions between conditional branches at these values.

A table mapping design requirements (rows) to test cases (columns). Any requirement with no test case has zero test coverage. Any test case not linked to a requirement may be unnecessary. The matrix ensures complete coverage and identifies gaps.

Spending more testing effort on high-risk areas: new code (more likely to have bugs), complex features (more interaction points), player-facing features (higher impact of failure), and areas with a history of bugs.

class_schedule.topic: Bug Reporting and Tracking with JIRA
learning_objectives: Document bugs with sufficient detail for reliable reproduction by developers
Use JIRA effectively for game QA workflows including custom fields and workflows
Classify bugs by severity, priority, and category using consistent standards
Manage bug lifecycle from discovery through verification and closure
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 8-9
Atlassian Jira Documentation - Agile Board Configuration
Best Practices for Bug Reporting in Game Development
How to Write a Bug Report That Gets Fixed (Mozilla Developer Guide)

lecture_notes.introduction: A bug report is a communication tool that must enable a developer to reproduce the issue without asking a single follow-up question. Every question a developer has to ask about a bug report is wasted time -- their time asking, your time answering, and the elapsed time between discovery and fix. A professional bug report contains: a clear summary that identifies the problem at a glance, the severity and priority correctly assessed, exact reproduction steps numbered sequentially, expected vs actual behavior explicitly stated, the environment (build version, hardware, OS), and evidence (screenshot, video, crash log). The difference between a good bug report and a bad one is the difference between a developer fixing the bug in 30 minutes and a developer spending 2 hours trying to reproduce it and then marking it 'cannot reproduce.' Today we master the art and science of bug reporting using JIRA as our tracking tool. We configure a game QA workflow in JIRA (Open > In Progress > Fixed > Verified > Closed, with Reopened and Won't Fix branches), create custom fields for game-specific metadata (build number, platform, reproduction rate, area/component), and practice writing bug reports for defects in a sample game. We also cover the bug lifecycle: how bugs flow through triage, assignment, fix, verification, and closure, and the QA tester's role at each stage.
key_concepts: Bug report anatomy: summary, severity, priority, reproduction steps, expected vs actual, environment, attachments, reproduction rate
Summary writing conventions: [Category] Brief description (e.g., '[Crash] Game crashes to desktop when opening inventory with 99+ items')
Reproduction steps: numbered, sequential, starting from a known state, each step a single action, reproducible by someone unfamiliar with the feature
Reproduction rate: X/Y format (3/5 = reproduced 3 out of 5 attempts), helps developers prioritize and estimate investigation difficulty
Evidence requirements: screenshot for visual bugs, video for behavioral/timing bugs, crash log for crashes, save file for save-related bugs
JIRA workflow for game QA: Open > In Progress > Fixed > Verified > Closed, with Reopened (fix failed), Won't Fix, and Deferred paths
Bug triage: a regular meeting where leads review new bugs, assign severity/priority, assign to developers, and defer low-priority issues
Duplicate detection: searching existing bugs before filing to avoid duplicates, linking related bugs for developer context
Regression flagging: marking bugs that were previously fixed and have reappeared, indicating a code regression
Bug metrics: open bug count by severity over time (burn-down), average time to fix, fix verification pass rate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Filing 5 bug reports for defects in a sample game build: a crash (with crash log), a visual glitch (with screenshot), a gameplay logic error (with video), an intermittent bug (with reproduction rate), and a performance issue (with profiler data). Each report follows professional conventions with all required fields. Then demonstrating the full lifecycle: bug is triaged, assigned to a developer, marked as fixed, verified by QA (one passes verification, one fails and is reopened).
setup_requirements: JIRA project configured with game QA workflow and custom fields
Sample game build with 5 known defects of varying types
OBS for screen/video recording of bugs
JIRA dashboard configured for bug tracking metrics

review.value: Express as X/Y (e.g., 3/5 means reproduced 3 out of 5 attempts). Always report this because it dramatically affects developer approach: a 5/5 bug can be debugged with breakpoints; a 1/50 bug may need logging instrumentation and statistical analysis.

Explicitly state both. 'Expected: Item is added to inventory. Actual: Item disappears and inventory count does not change.' Never assume the developer knows what the correct behavior should be -- they may not have read the design doc for this feature.

A meeting (typically daily or thrice-weekly) where leads review new bugs, confirm severity/priority, assign to developers, and make defer/fix/won't-fix decisions. Efficient triage prevents bugs from sitting unreviewed and ensures consistent classification.

After a developer marks a bug as Fixed, the original reporter (or another tester) verifies the fix on the new build. If the bug no longer reproduces, it is Closed. If it still reproduces, it is Reopened. This step prevents premature bug closure.

learning_objectives: Execute systematic functional testing of game features against design specifications
Apply exploratory testing techniques to discover defects beyond scripted test cases
Test game-specific systems: save/load, progression, economy, and UI state machines
Balance scripted and exploratory testing approaches for maximum coverage
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 10-11
Exploratory Testing (Bach & Bolton) Ch. 1-4
Agile Testing: A Practical Guide Ch. 7 - Exploratory Testing

lecture_notes.introduction: Functional testing verifies that every feature in the game works as specified in the design document. It is the foundation of game QA -- before you worry about performance, compatibility, or localization, the game must simply work correctly. Functional testing has two complementary approaches: scripted testing (executing predefined test cases step by step) and exploratory testing (intelligently investigating the game with a testing charter but without rigid scripts). Scripted testing provides measurable coverage and repeatability but can miss defects that fall between the cracks of predefined cases. Exploratory testing leverages the tester's creativity and domain knowledge to find unexpected bugs but is harder to measure and reproduce. Professional game QA uses both: scripted tests for critical paths and regression, exploratory sessions for discovery and edge cases. Game-specific functional areas require particular attention: save/load must preserve exact game state including entity positions, inventory contents, quest progress, and player stats. Economy systems must be tested for exploits (item duplication, currency overflow, negative price manipulation). Progression gates must be tested for sequence breaks (reaching a later area before completing prerequisites). UI state machines must handle rapid input, interruption, and concurrent operations. Today we execute functional testing on a sample game, alternating between scripted test case execution and time-boxed exploratory sessions, and comparing the types of defects each approach discovers.
key_concepts: Scripted testing: executing predefined test cases with documented steps, expected results, and pass/fail recording
Exploratory testing: simultaneous learning, test design, and execution guided by a charter (e.g., 'explore inventory edge cases for 30 minutes')
Session-based test management: time-boxed exploratory sessions with a charter, log, and debrief for measurable exploratory testing
Save/load testing: verifying all game state survives a save-load cycle including positions, inventory, progress, stats, and settings
Economy testing: checking for item duplication exploits, currency overflow/underflow, and unintended free resource generation
Progression sequence testing: attempting to reach later game stages by skipping prerequisites to find sequence break bugs
UI state machine testing: rapid input, menu interruption (pause during animation), concurrent operations, and back-navigation
Negative testing: deliberately providing invalid input, disconnecting during operations, and corrupting data to test error handling
Combinatorial testing: testing interactions between systems (equipping item while inventory is full, using ability while stunned)
Test session debriefing: after an exploratory session, documenting what was tested, what was found, and what areas need deeper investigation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Testing a sample RPG game build: (1) Scripted: execute 10 test cases for the inventory system covering add, remove, stack, split, sort, equip, and sell operations. (2) Exploratory 30-minute session with charter 'Investigate inventory edge cases and economy exploits.' Finding a item duplication exploit by dropping and picking up items during a save operation. (3) Debriefing the exploratory session: documenting the exploration path, bugs found, areas covered, and areas remaining.
setup_requirements: Sample game build with inventory, save/load, and economy systems
Test case suite for inventory functional testing
Exploratory testing charter and session log templates
JIRA for filing discovered bugs

review.value: A brief statement guiding an exploratory session: 'Explore [area] with focus on [concern] for [time duration].' Example: 'Explore the crafting system looking for item duplication exploits for 45 minutes.' Provides direction without constraining the tester's investigation.

Every piece of game state that matters to the player must survive a save-load cycle. Test by saving, noting exact state, loading, and comparing. Common failures: entity positions slightly off, temporary buffs lost, quest flag states reset, audio settings not persisted.

Reaching a later game area or state by bypassing intended prerequisites. In platformers: wall-clipping to skip a locked door. In RPGs: exploiting dialog trees to advance quests out of order. QA must actively try to break the intended progression.

Wrapping exploratory testing in measured sessions (typically 30-90 minutes) with a charter, real-time notes, and a debrief. Provides accountability and measurability to exploratory testing without constraining the tester's creative investigation.

learning_objectives: Design and maintain regression test suites that verify previously working features
Implement risk-based regression selection when full regression is not feasible
Create smoke test suites for rapid build verification
Track regression test effectiveness and coverage over time
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 12
Software Testing: Principles and Practices Ch. 8 - Regression Testing
Agile Testing: A Practical Guide Ch. 6 - Regression Strategies

lecture_notes.introduction: Every bug fix risks introducing a new bug. Every new feature can break existing features. Regression testing verifies that previously working functionality still works after code changes. In game development, regression testing is particularly challenging because games are complex interconnected systems: a change to the physics engine can affect combat, movement, puzzles, and particle effects simultaneously. A full regression test suite for a commercial game can take days to execute manually, which is impractical for daily or weekly builds. The solution is a tiered regression strategy. Smoke tests (10-30 minutes) verify the build is fundamentally stable: it launches, reaches the main menu, can start gameplay, and the core loop functions. These run on every build. Targeted regression (2-4 hours) tests the specific features and adjacent systems affected by recent code changes, guided by risk assessment of the changeset. Full regression (1-3 days) comprehensively retests all features and runs before major milestones, releases, and certification submissions. Today we design a three-tiered regression strategy for a sample game, create the smoke test suite, define targeted regression selection criteria based on code change analysis, and build a full regression suite from our existing test cases. We also discuss regression suite maintenance: removing obsolete tests, adding new tests for fixed bugs, and tracking the suite's effectiveness at catching regressions.
key_concepts: Regression testing purpose: verifying that code changes have not broken previously working functionality
Smoke test suite: 10-30 minute test set verifying critical path (launch > menu > gameplay > core loop > save/load)
Targeted regression: testing features specifically affected by recent code changes plus high-risk adjacent systems
Full regression: comprehensive retest of all features, run before milestones and release candidates
Risk-based selection: choosing which tests to run based on code change impact analysis and historical bug density
Regression candidate from bug fixes: every fixed bug should have a regression test case verifying the fix persists
Suite maintenance: removing tests for deleted features, updating tests for changed features, adding tests for new bugs
Build acceptance test (BAT): a synonym for smoke test, the minimum verification before any other testing begins
Regression effectiveness metric: the number of regressions caught by the suite divided by total regressions found (including escapes)
Continuous regression: integrating regression tests into CI/CD so they run automatically on every commit
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a three-tiered regression strategy: (1) Smoke suite: 15 test cases covering launch, menu navigation, new game start, core gameplay for 5 minutes, save, load, and exit. (2) Targeted regression: given a changeset that modified the combat damage formula, selecting test cases for damage dealing, damage receiving, status effects, healing, death, and difficulty scaling. (3) Full regression: organizing all 80+ test cases into an execution schedule with estimated duration and priority ordering.
setup_requirements: Sample game with existing test case library (80+ cases)
Test management tool with suite organization capability
Recent code changeset for targeted regression exercise
Spreadsheet template for regression planning

review.value: A smoke test must verify: the game launches without crashing, the main menu is functional, a new game can be started, core gameplay operates for at least 5 minutes, save and load complete without errors. If any of these fail, the build is rejected.

When a developer changes the physics system, test: character movement, jumping, collision, projectiles, ragdoll, destructible objects, and any feature that uses physics queries. The selection is based on dependency analysis: what systems call the changed code.
When a bug is fixed, create a test case that specifically verifies the fix. Include it in future regression suites. This prevents the same bug from silently reappearing when related code changes.

If the smoke test fails, the build is rejected and returned to development. No further testing is performed on a rejected build. This prevents testers from wasting time on a fundamentally broken build.

learning_objectives: Design and execute compatibility test matrices across hardware and software configurations
Identify platform-specific bugs caused by GPU vendors, drivers, OS versions, and input devices
Prioritize compatibility testing effort using market share data and risk analysis
Document compatibility issues with environment-specific reproduction steps
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 13
Steam Hardware Survey (latest data)
Unity Platform Compatibility Documentation
Cross-Platform Testing Strategy Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: A game that works perfectly on your development machine may crash on a player's computer with a different GPU, driver version, or OS configuration. Compatibility testing ensures the game works across the range of supported hardware and software configurations. The PC platform alone presents a staggering compatibility matrix: three GPU vendors (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel), dozens of GPU models per vendor, multiple driver versions per model, two major OS versions (Windows 10, Windows 11), several CPU architectures (Intel 12th-14th gen, AMD Ryzen 5000-7000), and various peripheral combinations (controllers, monitors, VR headsets, HOTAS). Mobile adds thousands of Android device configurations. Console is more controlled but still has hardware revisions and firmware versions. Testing every possible configuration is impossible, so compatibility testing is fundamentally about risk management: which configurations are most common (use Steam Hardware Survey and platform analytics), which are most likely to cause problems (new GPU architectures, oldest supported hardware, unusual aspect ratios), and which would cause the most damage if broken (configurations used by reviewers and streamers). Today we design a compatibility matrix for a PC game, prioritize configurations by market share and risk, execute compatibility tests on multiple hardware configurations, and document the environment-specific reproduction information that developers need to diagnose compatibility bugs.
key_concepts: Compatibility matrix dimensions: GPU vendor/model, GPU driver version, OS version, CPU architecture, RAM amount, storage type, input devices, display resolution/refresh rate
Minimum spec testing: verifying the game runs acceptably on the lowest supported hardware configuration
Maximum spec testing: verifying the game takes advantage of high-end hardware without artifacts from unused code paths
GPU vendor-specific bugs: shader compilation differences between NVIDIA, AMD, and Intel drivers that cause visual artifacts
Driver version testing: new GPU drivers can fix or introduce bugs; test on both latest and common older driver versions
Aspect ratio testing: 16:9, 21:9 (ultrawide), 32:9 (super ultrawide), 16:10, and 4:3 (legacy) may reveal UI layout or rendering bugs
Input device compatibility: keyboard/mouse, Xbox controller, PlayStation controller, Switch Pro controller, flight sticks, racing wheels
Steam Deck compatibility: a specific configuration with Linux/Proton, 800p display, APU graphics, and handheld input
Configuration prioritization: market share (Steam Survey) x risk level (new architecture) x impact (popular streamer hardware)
Environment documentation: exact GPU model, driver version, OS build number, and any relevant system settings in bug reports
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a compatibility matrix for a PC game: defining 12 priority configurations based on Steam Hardware Survey data (top 3 NVIDIA GPUs, top 2 AMD GPUs, Intel Arc, min spec, max spec, Steam Deck, ultrawide, 4K). Executing the smoke test suite on 3 different configurations (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel if available), identifying a shader compilation error on AMD hardware, and filing a compatibility bug report with exact driver version and GPU model.
setup_requirements: Access to at least 2 different hardware configurations (lab machines or cloud gaming PCs)
Steam Hardware Survey data for reference
Compatibility matrix spreadsheet template
Sample game build capable of running on multiple configurations

review.value: NVIDIA, AMD, and Intel GPUs interpret shader code slightly differently. A shader that compiles and runs correctly on NVIDIA may produce visual artifacts on AMD due to different precision handling, or fail to compile on Intel Arc. Always test on all three vendors.

Testing on the lowest hardware the game claims to support. If the store page says 'Minimum: GTX 960, 8GB RAM,' verify the game runs at acceptable frame rates and without crashes on exactly that hardware. False minimum specs generate angry reviews.

Steam Deck uses Linux with Proton (Windows compatibility layer), has a 1280x800 display, APU graphics, and handheld controls. Games must handle the small screen, gamepad-only input, and Proton compatibility. Valve's Deck Verified rating significantly affects sales.

Always include the exact GPU driver version (e.g., NVIDIA 546.33, AMD Adrenalin 24.1.1). A bug may only reproduce on specific driver versions. Without this information, the developer cannot set up the reproduction environment.

learning_objectives: Implement automated unit tests for game logic systems using Unity Test Framework
Create integration tests that verify interactions between game systems
Write Play Mode tests that validate runtime behavior in a scene
Design tests that are reliable, fast, and maintain value over time
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 14
Unity Test Framework Documentation
The Art of Unit Testing Ch. 1-5
Test-Driven Development for Games (GDC 2023 Talk)

lecture_notes.introduction: Manual testing does not scale. A manual regression suite that takes 8 hours to execute runs at most once per day. An automated test suite that takes 10 minutes to execute runs on every commit. Automation transforms regression testing from a bottleneck into a safety net. Game testing automation exists on three levels: unit tests verify individual functions and classes in isolation (does the damage formula calculate correctly?), integration tests verify that systems work together (does dealing damage to an enemy update the health bar and trigger the death animation when health reaches zero?), and end-to-end tests verify complete gameplay scenarios in a running game (can the player complete level 1?). Unity's Test Framework provides two test modes: Edit Mode tests run without starting the game (ideal for unit and integration tests of pure logic), and Play Mode tests run in a live game scene (ideal for testing MonoBehaviours, coroutines, and runtime behavior). Today we write tests at all three levels for a sample game's combat system: unit tests for the damage calculation formula, integration tests for the damage-health-death pipeline, and a Play Mode test that spawns an enemy, attacks it, and verifies it dies. We also discuss the test automation pyramid: many fast unit tests, fewer integration tests, and minimal end-to-end tests. Inverting this pyramid leads to slow, brittle, expensive suites.
key_concepts: Unit tests: testing individual functions and classes in isolation with mocked dependencies, running in milliseconds
Integration tests: testing interactions between systems without mocks, verifying the pipeline works end-to-end
Play Mode tests: running in a live Unity scene, testing MonoBehaviours, coroutines, physics, and rendering behavior
Edit Mode tests: running without starting the game, testing pure logic classes and ScriptableObjects
Test automation pyramid: many unit tests (fast, isolated) > fewer integration tests (moderate) > minimal E2E tests (slow, brittle)
Test isolation: each test must be independent, not relying on execution order or shared state from other tests
Mocking and stubbing: replacing real dependencies with controlled fakes for isolated testing (e.g., mock random number generator)
Assertion patterns: Assert.AreEqual, Assert.IsTrue, Assert.Throws for verifying expected outcomes
Test naming convention: MethodName_Condition_ExpectedResult (e.g., CalculateDamage_CriticalHit_ReturnsDoubleBaseDamage)
Test-driven development: writing the test first (it fails), writing the minimum code to make it pass, then refactoring
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing automated tests for a game combat system: (1) Unit test: CalculateDamage with base damage 10 and 2x multiplier returns 20. (2) Unit test: CalculateDamage with 0 defense target returns full damage. (3) Integration test: applying 100 damage to an entity with 50 HP reduces HP to 0 and sets isDead to true. (4) Play Mode test: spawning an enemy prefab, calling TakeDamage(100), waiting one frame, and verifying the death animation is playing. Running all tests in Unity Test Runner and showing pass/fail results.
setup_requirements: Unity project with Test Framework package installed
Sample game with combat system and death animations
Test assembly definitions for Edit Mode and Play Mode tests
NUnit attributes reference for Unity test assertions

review.key: Edit Mode vs Play Mode Tests
review.value: Edit Mode tests run instantly without starting the game engine, ideal for testing pure logic. Play Mode tests start a game scene and run over multiple frames, required for testing MonoBehaviours, coroutines, physics, and anything that depends on Unity's runtime.
MethodName_Scenario_ExpectedBehavior makes test names self-documenting. CalculateDamage_WithCriticalHit_ReturnsDoubleDamage tells you exactly what the test verifies without reading the code.

Each test must set up its own preconditions and tear down its own state. Tests must not depend on execution order. A test that passes when run alone but fails when run after another test has a shared state dependency bug.

Base: many fast unit tests (milliseconds each). Middle: fewer integration tests (seconds each). Top: minimal E2E tests (minutes each). Inverting this pyramid (many E2E, few unit tests) leads to slow suites, flaky tests, and long feedback cycles.

learning_objectives: Profile game performance to identify CPU, GPU, and memory bottlenecks
Establish automated performance benchmarks that detect regressions
Interpret profiling data to provide actionable optimization recommendations
Create performance test reports with frame time percentiles and trend analysis
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 15
Unity Profiler User Guide
Performance Testing Guidance for Web Applications (adapted for games)
GDC Vault: Performance Testing in AAA Games

lecture_notes.introduction: Performance is a feature. A game that stutters, has long load times, or runs at low frame rates on target hardware has a defect just as real as a crash or a visual glitch. Performance testing and profiling are the tools QA uses to detect these defects. Unlike functional testing where pass/fail is binary, performance testing is quantitative: you measure frame time, memory usage, load time, and other metrics, compare them against budgets, and track trends over time. A single frame time measurement is nearly useless -- what matters is the statistical distribution: average, median, 95th percentile (P95), 99th percentile (P99), and maximum. A game with a 16ms average frame time (60fps) but a 50ms P99 will feel stuttery because one in every hundred frames takes three times longer than the rest. Today we profile a sample game using Unity's Profiler for CPU and GPU timing, the Memory Profiler for heap and native memory analysis, and the Frame Debugger for GPU draw call investigation. We create an automated performance benchmark that runs a standardized gameplay sequence and captures frame time percentiles, then integrate it into CI so every build's performance is tracked over time. When a commit causes a P99 regression from 20ms to 35ms, the CI pipeline flags it before it reaches manual testing.
key_concepts: Frame time percentiles: P50 (median), P95, P99, P99.9, and max; each tells a different story about performance consistency
CPU profiling: identifying hot functions, GC allocation spikes, main thread blocking, and job system utilization
GPU profiling: draw call overhead, vertex processing, fragment shading, overdraw, and memory bandwidth
Memory profiling: managed heap size, native memory usage, texture memory, asset memory, and garbage collection frequency
Performance budgets: allocating frame time to subsystems (rendering 8ms, physics 2ms, scripts 2ms, headroom 4ms at 60fps)
Automated benchmarks: scripted gameplay sequences that produce reproducible frame time data for build-over-build comparison
Performance regression detection: comparing P95/P99 between builds and alerting when regression exceeds threshold (e.g., +3ms)
Load time measurement: time from game launch to interactive menu, from menu to gameplay, from death to respawn
Thermal/sustained performance: measuring frame time over 30+ minutes to detect thermal throttling (mobile/laptop)
Performance report format: summary table, frame time graph over benchmark duration, P50/P95/P99/Max, memory peak, and comparison to previous build
lecture_notes.demonstration: Profiling a sample game: (1) Running Unity Profiler during a 2-minute gameplay sequence, capturing CPU frame time, GPU frame time, and memory usage. (2) Identifying a 5ms GC spike every 3 seconds from per-frame string allocation. (3) Identifying GPU overdraw from overlapping particle systems. (4) Creating an automated benchmark using a recorded input playback that runs the same sequence reproducibly. (5) Running the benchmark on two builds and comparing P95 frame times to detect a regression.
setup_requirements: Unity project with Profiler and Memory Profiler packages
Sample game build with performance issues (GC spikes, overdraw)
Benchmark recording system (input playback or scripted sequence)
Spreadsheet or dashboard for performance trend tracking

review.value: The frame time exceeded by only 1% of frames. If P99 is 30ms but average is 10ms, the game stutters noticeably once every 100 frames (about every 1.7 seconds at 60fps). P99 is more important than average for perceiving smoothness.

Look for periodic CPU spikes in the Profiler timeline. If spikes occur at regular intervals, they are likely garbage collection. The GC Alloc column shows per-frame allocation. Any allocation in the update loop should be investigated and eliminated.

A scripted gameplay sequence that runs identically on every build, producing frame time data for comparison. Detects performance regressions from code changes before they reach manual testing. Must control for variable inputs (scripted AI, fixed seed).
The CI pipeline runs the benchmark on every merge to main. If any subsystem exceeds its budget (e.g., rendering > 10ms) or P99 regresses by more than 3ms, the merge is flagged for investigation.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between QA testing (finding bugs) and playtesting (evaluating design)
Design and conduct structured playtesting sessions with appropriate methodology
Collect and analyze qualitative and quantitative player feedback
Translate playtesting findings into actionable design recommendations
readings: Games User Research Ch. 4-8
The Art of Game Design (Schell) Ch. 26 - Playtesting
Quantitative Methods in Games User Research (GDC 2022)

lecture_notes.introduction: QA testing asks 'does this work correctly?' Playtesting asks 'is this fun?' Both are essential, but they require different mindsets, methodologies, and participants. A QA tester systematically executes test cases against design specifications and reports deviations. A playtester represents the target audience and provides data about the player experience: is the tutorial clear? Is the difficulty appropriate? Is the progression motivating? Do controls feel responsive? These are design questions that cannot be answered by looking at code or reading design documents -- they can only be answered by observing real players. Today we cover the three types of playtesting: usability testing (can the player figure out what to do and how to do it?), focus testing (does the player enjoy the experience and want to continue?), and balance testing (are difficulty curves, progression rates, and competitive matchups fair?). We learn the think-aloud protocol (having players verbalize their thought process during play), biometric measurement (heart rate, skin conductance for emotional engagement), and quantitative analytics (heatmaps, funnel analysis, session length). We conduct a live playtesting session with a classmate as participant, applying proper research methodology: neutral facilitation, non-leading observation, and rigorous data collection.
key_concepts: Usability testing: can the player figure out what to do and how to do it without external help?
Focus testing: does the player enjoy the experience and want to continue playing?
Balance testing: are difficulty curves fair, is progression satisfying, are competitive matchups balanced?
Think-aloud protocol: participants verbalize their thoughts, feelings, and reasoning during gameplay
Neutral facilitation: never leading the player, never expressing approval or disapproval, using open-ended prompts
Observation without intervention: watching the player struggle without helping, because the struggle is the data
Post-session questionnaire: standardized rating scales for satisfaction, difficulty, clarity, and likelihood to continue
Quantitative playtesting analytics: heatmaps (death locations, movement patterns), funnel analysis (progression drop-off), session metrics
Recruitment and sample size: 5 participants catch 80% of major usability issues, 15+ needed for quantitative insights
Actionable recommendations: translating observations into specific, prioritized design changes with expected impact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running a live moderated playtest: (1) Brief the participant (we are testing the game, not you). (2) Record screen and audio. (3) Observe think-aloud gameplay for 15 minutes without intervening. (4) Note timestamped observations (confusion points, frustration, delight, error recovery). (5) Administer post-session questionnaire. (6) Conduct 5-minute semi-structured interview. (7) Analyze data and produce 3 prioritized design recommendations.
setup_requirements: Playable game build at a testable milestone
OBS configured for screen and webcam recording
Playtesting consent form and questionnaire templates
Observation note-taking template with timestamp column

review.value: The participant narrates their thoughts during gameplay: 'I think I need to go left... this door is locked, maybe I need a key... I do not see any key, I will try the other path.' If silent for 30+ seconds, prompt with 'What are you thinking right now?' -- never 'Have you tried the key?'

Players behave differently when watched. Mitigate by: making the player comfortable with small talk first, emphasizing you are testing the game not them, using unobtrusive recording, and allowing 2-3 minutes of unobserved warm-up.

Research shows 5 participants discover approximately 80% of major usability problems. Beyond 5, each additional participant finds fewer new issues. For quantitative data (metrics, A/B tests), 15-30 participants are needed.

A finding is not a recommendation. Finding: '4 of 5 players did not notice the key on the shelf.' Recommendation: 'Add a visual highlight (glow or sparkle) to the key and a voice line drawing attention to the shelf.' Specify the change, not just the problem.

learning_objectives: Execute localization testing for text, audio, and cultural appropriateness
Identify common localization bugs: truncation, encoding, placeholder, and context errors
Test right-to-left (RTL) language support and CJK character rendering
Validate linguistic accuracy with native speaker review workflows
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 16
The Game Localization Handbook Ch. 5-8
Unicode and Internationalization for Game Developers

lecture_notes.introduction: Localization testing verifies that a game works correctly and appropriately in every supported language and region. It goes far beyond checking translations -- it tests whether the UI can accommodate text that is 30% longer in German, whether Arabic text renders right-to-left correctly, whether Japanese characters display without font fallback issues, whether date and number formats match regional conventions, and whether content is culturally appropriate for each market. Localization bugs are among the most common causes of negative reviews in international markets, and they are entirely preventable with proper testing. A button labeled 'Start Game' that overflows its container when translated to 'Spiel Starten' in German looks unprofessional. A tutorial that references 'pressing X' when the Japanese version uses circle for confirm is confusing. A character name that is offensive in Portuguese drives an entire market away. Today we execute localization testing on a sample game with three language versions: English, German (Latin script with text expansion), and Japanese (CJK characters). We test text rendering, UI layout accommodation, placeholder substitution, date/number formatting, and cultural content review. We use pseudo-localization as a development tool to catch localization bugs before translations are even available.
key_concepts: Text expansion: translated text is often longer than English (German +30%, Finnish +40%), requiring flexible UI layouts
Text truncation: when translated text exceeds UI container size, it gets cut off, hiding information from the player
Pseudo-localization: automatically replacing English text with accented/expanded versions to test UI flexibility before real translations arrive
Placeholder testing: verifying that string substitution variables ({0}, {name}, %d) work correctly in all languages and word orders
RTL layout: Arabic, Hebrew, and other right-to-left languages require mirrored UI layouts and reversed text flow
CJK rendering: Chinese, Japanese, Korean characters require specific fonts, line-breaking rules, and often larger font sizes
Cultural sensitivity: symbols, colors, gestures, and content that are acceptable in one culture may be offensive in another
Date and number formatting: 01/02/2024 means January 2 in the US but February 1 in most of Europe; 1,000 vs 1.000
Font coverage: ensuring the game's fonts contain all characters needed for every supported language (check for missing glyphs)
Audio localization: verifying dubbed audio matches subtitles, lip sync is acceptable, and audio file references are correct per language
lecture_notes.demonstration: Testing a sample game in three languages: (1) English baseline verification. (2) German: finding text truncation in the main menu button, placeholder error in a quest description, and a UI element that cannot accommodate the longer translation. (3) Japanese: finding a missing glyph in the score font and a text string that was not extracted for translation (hardcoded in code). (4) Running pseudo-localization on the English build to proactively identify more truncation issues.
setup_requirements: Sample game build with English, German, and Japanese localization
Pseudo-localization tool or script
Font coverage verification tool
Localization testing checklist template

review.value: Replacing 'Start Game' with '[Staaart Gaamee !!!]' -- accented characters test encoding, extra length tests UI flexibility, brackets test text extraction, and exclamation marks fill expansion. Catches layout bugs before real translations exist.

Text written directly in code rather than loaded from a localization file. These strings cannot be translated and appear in English in all language versions. QA must flag every visible English string in a non-English build.

In English: '{0} defeated {1}.' In Japanese, the subject comes last: '{1} was defeated by {0}.' If the localization system does not support reordering placeholders, Japanese players see nonsensical text.

When a font does not contain a required character, the system substitutes from a fallback font. The fallback may have a different size, weight, or style, creating visually jarring mixed text. Test by checking every supported language for glyph coverage.

learning_objectives: Understand platform certification processes for Xbox, PlayStation, Nintendo, and mobile app stores
Execute compliance testing against certification requirement checklists
Identify and resolve common certification failure reasons before submission
Prepare submission packages that pass certification on the first attempt
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 17-18
Xbox Requirements Documentation (XR overview)
PlayStation Technical Requirements Checklist (TRC overview)
Apple App Store Review Guidelines - Games Section

lecture_notes.introduction: Before a game reaches players on any major platform, it must pass certification. Sony, Microsoft, and Nintendo each maintain hundreds of specific technical requirements covering everything from save data behavior during power loss to controller button prompt accuracy. Apple and Google have their own review guidelines for mobile. Failing certification delays your launch by weeks or months and costs significant money in resubmission fees and lost revenue. QA's job is to ensure the game passes on the first attempt by running an internal certification pass before official submission. Today we study the major certification frameworks: Xbox Requirements (XR), PlayStation Technical Requirements Checklist (TRC), Nintendo Lotcheck, Apple App Store Review Guidelines, and Google Play policies. While the specific requirements are confidential and change regularly, the categories are public: system integration (proper use of platform APIs), user experience (correct button prompts, accessibility features), data management (save integrity, cloud sync), network behavior (graceful disconnection, content filtering), and content guidelines (age rating, regional restrictions). We perform an internal certification pass on a sample game build, testing common failure areas: verifying button prompts match the active input device, testing save data integrity after simulated power loss, verifying graceful network disconnection handling, and checking that all text respects platform-specific terminology requirements.
key_concepts: Xbox Requirements (XR): Microsoft's certification framework covering system API usage, user experience, and content standards
PlayStation TRC: Sony's Technical Requirements Checklist with specific tests for save data, user interface, and system features
Nintendo Lotcheck: Nintendo's certification process covering hardware compliance and content guidelines
Apple App Store Review: human review covering crashes, monetization, privacy, and content with 24-48 hour turnaround
Google Play Review: automated and human review covering policy compliance, permissions, and content ratings
Button prompt accuracy: displaying the correct controller button icons for the active input device (Xbox, PlayStation, Switch, keyboard)
Save data integrity: game saves must survive interrupted writes (power loss, crash) using atomic write patterns
Network disconnection handling: graceful error messages and state recovery when network is lost during online gameplay
Platform-specific terminology: using 'Achievement' on Xbox, 'Trophy' on PlayStation, and correct button names per platform
Internal certification pass: running a simplified version of platform certification tests internally before official submission
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performing an internal certification pass: (1) Button prompt test: connecting Xbox, PlayStation, and keyboard inputs and verifying displayed prompts change correctly for each. (2) Save integrity test: saving during gameplay, simulating power loss by killing the process, and verifying the save loads correctly or gracefully falls back to the previous save. (3) Network test: disconnecting during an online operation and verifying a user-facing error message appears without crashing. (4) Terminology test: checking all UI text for platform-specific terms.
setup_requirements: Sample game build with save system and network features
Multiple input devices (Xbox controller, PlayStation controller, keyboard)
Internal certification checklist (simplified from platform requirements)
Network simulation tools for disconnection testing

review.value: Write save data to a temporary file, then rename the temporary file to the actual save file. If power is lost during writing, the temporary file is incomplete but the previous save is intact. The rename operation is atomic on most filesystems.

When a player switches from controller to keyboard (or vice versa), all on-screen prompts must immediately update. Showing 'Press A' when using a keyboard, or 'Press X' when using an Xbox controller on PlayStation, is a certification failure.

Incorrect button prompts (40% of first-submission failures), save data corruption on power loss (25%), crashing during network disconnection (20%), and missing accessibility features required by platform (15%). All are preventable.

A certification failure means fixing the issues, resubmitting, and waiting another review cycle (1-3 weeks). For time-sensitive launches (holiday season, marketing windows), this delay can cost millions in revenue. First-attempt pass is critical.

learning_objectives: Test game accessibility against established guidelines and checklists
Evaluate games for visual, auditory, motor, and cognitive accessibility
Use assistive technologies and simulation tools to test accessibility features
Provide accessibility feedback that is actionable for the development team
readings: Game Accessibility Guidelines (gameaccessibilityguidelines.com)
Xbox Accessibility Guidelines (XAGs)
The Last of Us Part II Accessibility Feature Analysis
AbleGamers Includification Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Approximately 15-20% of gamers have some form of disability. Accessibility testing ensures that games are playable by the widest possible audience. Unlike most other testing types where there is a clear pass/fail, accessibility testing evaluates how well the game accommodates different abilities, with recommendations ranging from basic (minimum playability) to advanced (full feature access). Major platforms now require or strongly incentivize accessibility features: Xbox Accessibility Guidelines (XAGs) are part of certification, PlayStation has accessibility certification requirements, and the CVAA (Communications and Video Accessibility Act) has legal implications for online games with communication features. The game industry has made remarkable progress: The Last of Us Part II shipped with over 60 accessibility options and won a BAFTA for its accessibility design. Today we test a game against the four categories of accessibility: visual (colorblind modes, text scaling, high contrast, screen reader support), auditory (subtitles, visual audio cues, haptic feedback for audio events), motor (remappable controls, one-handed play, adjustable timing, auto-aim), and cognitive (difficulty options, simplified UI modes, tutorial replay, clear objectives). We use simulation tools to experience the game as a player with different abilities would, identifying barriers that might not be obvious to an able-bodied tester.
key_concepts: Visual accessibility: colorblind modes (protanopia, deuteranopia, tritanopia), scalable text, high-contrast UI, screen magnification
Auditory accessibility: subtitles with speaker identification, visual indicators for directional audio cues, haptic alternatives
Motor accessibility: fully remappable controls, one-handed play options, adjustable button hold vs toggle, auto-aim and aim assist
Cognitive accessibility: multiple difficulty levels, clear objective markers, tutorial replay, simplified UI option, adjustable game speed
Colorblind simulation tools: applying color filters to the game output to see what colorblind players experience
Xbox Accessibility Guidelines (XAGs): 23 guidelines covering text, contrast, audio, input, difficulty, and communication accessibility
WCAG applicability: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines principles (perceivable, operable, understandable, robust) applied to games
Accessibility testing checklist: systematic evaluation of each accessibility category with pass/partial/fail assessment
Assistive technology testing: testing with screen readers, switch controls, and eye tracking input devices
Inclusive design vs retrofit: designing for accessibility from the start (inclusive) vs adding features later (retrofit)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Testing a sample game for accessibility: (1) Visual: applying a deuteranopia color simulation filter and checking if all color-coded gameplay information is still distinguishable. Finding that red and green health/damage indicators are indistinguishable. (2) Auditory: muting all audio and playing for 5 minutes, noting any gameplay information conveyed only through sound. Finding that an enemy attack audio cue has no visual equivalent. (3) Motor: attempting to play with one hand, identifying which actions require simultaneous button presses. (4) Cognitive: evaluating whether the tutorial can be replayed and objectives are clearly marked.
setup_requirements: Sample game build with some accessibility features implemented
Colorblind simulation tool or filter
Game accessibility guidelines checklist
Headphones (for auditory testing) and method to mute audio (for visual-only testing)

review.value: 8% of males and 0.5% of females have some form of color vision deficiency. Test with simulation filters for protanopia (red-blind), deuteranopia (green-blind), and tritanopia (blue-blind). Any information conveyed solely through red/green distinction is a failure.

Subtitles should include speaker identification, be scalable in size, have a background for contrast, and be available for all dialogue and important audio. Closed captions additionally describe sound effects and music cues.

Players must be able to reassign every game action to any available button. This accommodates custom controller setups, one-handed play, and assistive devices. Platform certification increasingly requires full remapping support.

Xbox's 23 Accessibility Guidelines cover text readability, contrast ratios, audio alternatives, input customization, difficulty options, and communication accessibility. Meeting XAGs is becoming a certification factor and a marketing differentiator.

learning_objectives: Configure continuous integration pipelines that build and test game projects automatically
Integrate automated tests, linting, and performance benchmarks into CI workflows
Design deployment pipelines for internal testing, QA, and release builds
Troubleshoot CI-specific issues: build machines, license management, and long build times
readings: Continuous Integration for Games (GDC 2023 Talk Notes)
GitHub Actions Documentation
Unity Build Automation Documentation
Jenkins for Game Development (tutorial series)

lecture_notes.introduction: Continuous integration transforms QA from a phase into a process. Instead of testing a monolithic build at the end of a sprint, CI tests every commit continuously, catching bugs within minutes of introduction. A well-configured CI pipeline for a game project builds the project on every push, runs all automated tests (unit, integration, Play Mode), checks for code quality issues (linting, static analysis), runs performance benchmarks against budgets, produces distributable builds for QA and stakeholders, and notifies the team when anything fails. The result is that a developer who introduces a bug gets feedback within 15 minutes instead of 2 weeks. Today we configure a complete CI/CD pipeline for a Unity game using GitHub Actions: a workflow that triggers on every push to main and every pull request, checks out the code, activates a Unity license, builds for the target platform, runs all test assemblies, captures performance benchmark results, and uploads the build artifact. We also discuss the game-specific CI challenges: Unity builds require a license (self-hosted runners or Unity Build Automation), build times for large projects can exceed 30 minutes, Play Mode tests require a display (use virtual frame buffer on Linux), and asset import can be slow without caching. We address these with build caching, parallelized workflows, and self-hosted runners.
key_concepts: CI pipeline stages: checkout > license activation > build > test > benchmark > artifact upload > notification
GitHub Actions for Unity: using GameCI or custom Docker images with Unity installed for automated builds
Unity Test Framework in CI: running Edit Mode and Play Mode tests headlessly with JUnit-format result output
Build caching: caching the Library folder between runs to avoid re-importing assets (saves 10-30 minutes per build)
Self-hosted runners: dedicated build machines with Unity licenses for faster builds and persistent caches
Branch protection rules: requiring CI tests to pass before merging pull requests into the main branch
Build artifact management: storing successful builds as downloadable artifacts for QA access and archival
Nightly builds: scheduled CI runs that produce QA builds even on days with no code changes, for regression testing
Build notification: Slack, Discord, or email alerts when builds fail, with links to logs and test results
Pipeline optimization: parallelizing platform builds, splitting test suites across multiple runners, incremental builds
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a GitHub Actions CI pipeline for a Unity project: (1) Creating a workflow YAML that triggers on push and PR. (2) Using GameCI's Unity Docker image with license activation. (3) Running Edit Mode and Play Mode tests with JUnit output. (4) Building for Windows with the Library folder cached for fast subsequent builds. (5) Uploading the build as an artifact. (6) Configuring branch protection to require CI pass before merge. Testing the pipeline by pushing a commit with a failing test and observing the PR status check fail.
setup_requirements: Unity project with automated tests in a GitHub repository
GitHub Actions enabled on the repository
Unity license for CI builds (personal or professional)
GameCI GitHub Actions or equivalent Unity build action

review.value: Unity's Library folder contains imported and processed versions of all assets. Without caching, every CI build re-imports all assets from scratch (10-30 minutes for medium projects). Caching the Library folder between runs reduces subsequent build times to minutes.

GitHub's branch protection rules can require that all CI status checks pass before a pull request can be merged. This prevents broken code from reaching the main branch. Combined with automated tests, it creates an automated quality gate.

Play Mode tests require a display. On Linux CI servers without a monitor, use Xvfb (virtual frame buffer) to provide a virtual display. On Windows servers, configure the Unity player to run in batch mode with a virtual display.

When CI fails, the team must know immediately. Configure Slack/Discord/email notifications for failed builds with links to logs. A build failure that sits unnoticed for hours defeats the purpose of CI.

learning_objectives: Manage QA processes, schedules, and resources across a game project lifecycle
Track and report QA metrics that provide actionable insights to stakeholders
Conduct risk assessments to guide testing prioritization at each project phase
Communicate QA status and release readiness to non-technical stakeholders
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 19-20
Managing Software Testing (Dust, Rashka, McDermid) Ch. 5-8
GDC Vault: QA Metrics That Actually Matter

lecture_notes.introduction: QA management is the discipline of running the QA team and process effectively: planning test activities, allocating resources, tracking progress, communicating status, and making the release/no-release recommendation. The QA manager (or lead) must answer several questions for stakeholders at any point in the project: How much have we tested? How many bugs remain? Are we on track to hit the release date? What are the highest risks right now? The answers come from metrics. But not all metrics are useful -- some are actively misleading. A high bug count might mean the game is buggy or might mean QA is doing an excellent job finding bugs. A low bug count might mean the game is solid or might mean QA is not looking hard enough. The metrics that actually matter are: defect discovery rate trend (are we finding bugs faster or slower than we are fixing them?), test coverage by risk area (are the highest-risk features adequately tested?), defect escape rate (are bugs reaching players that QA should have caught?), and release readiness criteria (have we met the predefined exit criteria?). Today we build a QA dashboard with meaningful metrics, practice estimating test effort for upcoming features, conduct a risk assessment for a game approaching release, and write a release readiness report that communicates QA status to non-technical stakeholders.
key_concepts: QA planning: estimating test effort for features based on complexity, risk, and historical data (hours per test case, bugs per feature)
Resource allocation: distributing testers across feature areas based on risk, deadline, and tester expertise
Bug burn-down chart: tracking open bugs by severity over time; the curve should trend downward toward release
Defect discovery vs fix rate: if new bugs are found faster than they are fixed, the project is diverging from release readiness
Test coverage dashboard: percentage of test cases executed and passed by feature area, highlighting under-tested areas
Release readiness criteria: predefined conditions that must be met for a release go/no-go decision (zero Critical, < 5 Major, 95% P1 pass rate)
Risk assessment matrix: probability x impact scoring for untested features, known issues, and environmental risks
QA status report: a regular communication (daily or weekly) summarizing testing progress, top risks, and blocker bugs
Retrospective: post-release analysis of QA effectiveness, identifying process improvements for the next project
Vanity metrics to avoid: total bug count (meaningless without context), test case count (quantity is not quality), hours tested (effort is not effectiveness)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a QA dashboard for a game approaching release: (1) Bug burn-down chart showing open Critical, Major, and Minor bugs over the last 4 weeks. (2) Test coverage table by feature area (combat 95%, inventory 88%, multiplayer 72%, achievements 45%). (3) Release readiness scorecard (zero Critical: PASS, < 5 Major: FAIL with 7 open, P1 test pass rate 93%: FAIL). (4) Risk assessment flagging multiplayer (low coverage + high player impact) as the top risk. (5) Writing a release readiness summary for stakeholders: 'QA recommends a 1-week delay to address 7 open Major bugs and complete multiplayer testing.'
setup_requirements: JIRA project with bug data spanning multiple weeks
Test management tool with coverage tracking
Dashboard tool (JIRA dashboard, Google Sheets, or Grafana)
Release readiness report template

review.value: A chart tracking open bug count over time by severity. A healthy project shows a downward trend as the release approaches. If the curve is flat or increasing, bugs are being found faster than fixed, and the release date is at risk.

Predefined conditions agreed upon before testing begins. Example: zero Critical bugs, fewer than 5 Major bugs, 95% of P1 test cases pass, all certification requirements verified. If criteria are not met, the release is delayed.

Bugs found by players that QA should have caught, divided by total bugs. A 10% escape rate means 1 in 10 bugs reaches players. Tracking this over releases reveals whether QA coverage is improving or degrading.

When time is limited, test the highest-risk areas first. Risk = probability of bugs (new code, complex features, recent changes) x impact of bugs (player-facing, revenue-affecting, certification-blocking). A risk matrix guides where to spend limited testing time.

learning_objectives: Present a comprehensive QA test plan for a class game project
Demonstrate the complete QA workflow: planning, execution, automation, and reporting
Articulate QA findings with evidence and actionable recommendations
Evaluate peer QA work against industry standards
readings: QA Presentation Best Practices
Game QA Portfolio Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Today each student presents their final project: a comprehensive QA pass on a class game project. Your presentation demonstrates the complete QA lifecycle: test planning (what you tested and why), test execution (scripted and exploratory results), automated testing (the test suite you built and integrated into CI), performance analysis (profiling data and benchmark results), and a release readiness assessment (your professional recommendation on whether the game is ready to ship). This is not a theoretical exercise -- you are presenting real testing of a real game build with real bugs you found, real tests you automated, and real performance data you collected. Your presentation should convince the audience that you understand QA not as a role that finds bugs, but as a discipline that ensures quality. The difference between a junior tester and a senior QA professional is the ability to plan systematically, prioritize by risk, communicate clearly, and provide the development team with the information they need to make confident release decisions. Your post-mortem should reflect on what QA activities were most valuable, what you would do differently, and what you learned about the relationship between QA and development.
key_concepts: Comprehensive QA presentation: test plan, execution results, automation, performance data, and release recommendation
Evidence-based reporting: every finding backed by screenshots, profiler data, or test results
Release readiness recommendation: a clear go/no-go/conditional recommendation with supporting criteria
Automation demonstration: running the automated test suite live and showing CI pipeline results
Performance summary: frame time percentiles, memory peak, and comparison to performance budget
Bug portfolio: selection of highest-impact bugs found, demonstrating bug reporting professionalism
Process reflection: which testing activities provided the most value per hour invested
Peer evaluation: evaluating classmates' QA work for thoroughness, professionalism, and effectiveness
Portfolio preparation: framing QA work for job applications in game studios
Industry readiness: demonstrating the skills expected of an entry-level game QA professional
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student presents for 8-10 minutes with 3-5 minutes Q&A. Presentations include a walkthrough of the test plan, key bug findings with evidence, a live run of the automated test suite, performance profiling summary, and a release readiness recommendation with justification.
setup_requirements: Presentation laptop with access to JIRA, test management tool, and CI dashboard
Game build for live automation demonstration
Presentation slides with profiling data, metrics, and bug evidence
Written post-mortem and release readiness report submitted before presentation

review.value: The final deliverable of a QA pass: a clear statement of whether the game meets release criteria, with supporting evidence. 'QA recommends release with 2 known shippable issues' or 'QA recommends delaying release to fix 3 Critical bugs affecting certification.'

Select 5-8 bugs that demonstrate range (different severities, types, and feature areas) and professionalism (clear reproduction steps, appropriate evidence, correct classification). This is the most portable artifact for job applications.
Demonstrating that your automated tests catch real bugs (not just exercise code paths) and run in CI on every commit. Show a test that would have caught a regression you found manually.

A game QA portfolio should include: test plans, bug report samples, automated test code, performance analysis, and a release readiness report. These artifacts demonstrate the systematic, evidence-based approach that studios value.

quizzes.topic: QA Fundamentals, Test Planning, and Bug Reporting

questions.question: A tester files the following bug report: 'The game crashes sometimes when I open the inventory.' Identify four specific improvements that would make this report actionable for a developer, and rewrite the summary line following professional conventions.
questions.answer: Four improvements: (1) Add exact reproduction steps (Step 1: Start new game, Step 2: Collect 15+ items from the first dungeon, Step 3: Press Tab to open inventory). (2) Include reproduction rate (e.g., 3/10 attempts). (3) Specify the environment: OS version, hardware specs, game build number, and relevant settings. (4) Attach evidence: crash log file, video of the crash, and screenshot of any error dialog. Rewritten summary: '[Crash] Game crashes to desktop when opening inventory with 15+ items (3/10 repro, Build 2024.1.15, Win11 x64)'.

rubric.key: Identifies need for specific numbered reproduction steps
Identifies need for reproduction rate and environment details
Rewritten summary follows professional conventions with key details

questions.question: Explain the difference between severity and priority in bug classification. Give an example of a bug that is high severity but low priority, and one that is low severity but high priority.
questions.answer: Severity measures the impact on the player experience. Priority measures how urgently it should be fixed. They are independent: severity is objective (how bad is it?), priority is a business decision (how soon must we fix it?). High severity, low priority: the final boss has a rare crash (1/100) on a specific GPU model used by 2% of players. The crash is critical severity (data loss, blocks progression) but low priority because it affects few players and the game launches in 3 months with time for a patch. Low severity, high priority: the company logo on the title screen is misspelled. The bug is trivial severity (cosmetic text error) but high priority because the game launches tomorrow and the logo is in every screenshot, trailer, and marketing material.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes severity (impact) from priority (urgency)
Valid high-severity low-priority example with reasoning
Valid low-severity high-priority example with reasoning

questions.question: What is boundary value analysis and why are most bugs found at boundaries? Provide an example for a game feature with a 0-99 valid range.
questions.answer: Boundary value analysis tests at the exact edges of valid input ranges and one step beyond. Most bugs cluster at boundaries because code transitions between conditional branches at these values (e.g., if count >= max). Off-by-one errors, incorrect comparison operators (< vs <=), and type overflow are all boundary bugs. For a 0-99 valid range (e.g., inventory slots), test: -1 (below minimum, should be rejected), 0 (minimum valid), 1 (just above minimum), 98 (just below maximum), 99 (maximum valid), 100 (above maximum, should be rejected). Common boundary bugs: inventory accepts item 100 (off-by-one in limit check), inventory crashes with 0 items (empty collection not handled), or negative index from -1 input causes array out-of-bounds.

rubric.key: Correctly defines boundary value analysis
Explains why bugs cluster at boundaries
Provides correct boundary test values for 0-99 range

quizzes.topic: Functional Testing, Regression Testing, and Compatibility

questions.question: Explain the difference between a smoke test and a full regression test suite. Describe when you would run each and what happens if you skip smoke testing on a new build.
questions.answer: A smoke test is a small, fast set of tests (10-30 minutes) verifying the most critical functionality: the game launches, reaches the main menu, can start gameplay, and the core loop functions without crashes. A full regression suite comprehensively retests all features and previously fixed bugs, taking hours or days. Smoke tests run on every new build before any other testing begins. Full regression runs at major milestones, before release candidates, and after significant code merges. Skipping smoke testing: if you proceed directly to full regression or feature testing on an untested build, testers may waste hours on a fundamentally broken build (one that crashes on startup or has corrupted data). All their test results are invalidated when the build is rejected. Smoke testing takes 15 minutes and saves hours of wasted effort.

rubric.key: Correctly defines smoke test with scope and timing
Correctly defines full regression with scope and timing
Clear consequence of skipping smoke tests

questions.question: A game developer changed the physics engine's collision detection code. As the QA lead, describe how you would select a targeted regression test set for this change.
questions.answer: Targeted regression selection by dependency analysis: collision detection is used by every system that queries physics. Test: (1) Character controller: walking into walls, standing on slopes, climbing steps, falling through floors. (2) Projectiles: bullets hitting targets, arrows sticking to surfaces, grenades bouncing. (3) Vehicles: car-wall collisions, wheel-ground contact, passenger collision. (4) Destructibles: breaking objects on impact, debris collision with player. (5) Pickups: player walking over collectible items, item drop landing on surfaces. (6) AI pathfinding: NPC navigation around obstacles if path queries use collision. (7) Triggers: walking through trigger volumes for doors, checkpoints, cutscenes. Priority: test character controller first (highest player impact), then projectiles (core combat), then everything else.

rubric.key: Identifies multiple systems that depend on collision detection
Provides specific test scenarios for the highest-priority systems
Prioritizes by player impact

questions.question: Why is GPU vendor diversity important in compatibility testing? Name the three major GPU vendors and describe a type of bug that would only appear on one vendor's hardware.
questions.answer: GPU vendor diversity is important because each vendor's GPU driver interprets shader code differently, handles edge cases in graphics APIs differently, and has different performance characteristics. A game tested only on NVIDIA hardware may have bugs visible only on AMD or Intel GPUs. The three major vendors: NVIDIA (GeForce), AMD (Radeon), Intel (Arc). A vendor-specific bug example: a shader that uses an uninitialized variable compiles and runs correctly on NVIDIA (which happens to zero-initialize) but produces visual garbage on AMD (which does not zero-initialize). The shader code is technically incorrect on all platforms, but the bug only manifests visually on AMD because of different driver behavior regarding undefined values.

rubric.key: Explains why vendor diversity matters (different driver behavior)
Names all three major GPU vendors correctly
Describes a plausible vendor-specific bug with technical reasoning
