quizzes.topic: Automated Testing, Performance Profiling, and Playtesting

questions.question: A game has an average frame rate of 58fps but players complain it feels stuttery. The developer insists performance is fine because the average is near 60fps. Explain what metric the developer should examine and what it would likely reveal.
questions.answer: The developer should examine frame time percentiles, specifically the 99th percentile (P99) frame time and frame time variance. Average FPS of 58 could mask severe inconsistency: 95% of frames might render in 10ms (100fps) while 5% spike to 80ms (12.5fps) due to garbage collection pauses or asset streaming hitches. The P99 frame time would reveal these spikes. A frame time graph over time would show periodic patterns (suggesting GC allocation cycles) or situational spikes (suggesting specific gameplay events triggering expensive operations). The stuttery 58fps average likely has a P99 above 30ms with periodic frame time spikes. The fix involves eliminating the source of the spikes (per-frame allocations, synchronous asset loading) rather than optimizing average performance.

rubric.key: Identifies P99 frame time as the correct metric
Explains how average FPS masks periodic spikes
Identifies likely causes of the spikes

questions.question: Describe the test automation pyramid and explain why inverting it (many E2E tests, few unit tests) is problematic for game QA.
questions.answer: The test automation pyramid has three layers: base (many fast unit tests that take milliseconds each), middle (fewer integration tests that take seconds each), and top (minimal end-to-end tests that take minutes each). Inverting the pyramid is problematic because: (1) Slow feedback: E2E tests that launch the game, play through scenarios, and verify outcomes take minutes each. A suite of 100 E2E tests could take hours, meaning developers wait hours for CI results. (2) Brittleness: E2E tests depend on many systems working correctly simultaneously. A minor UI change can break dozens of E2E tests that are not testing UI. (3) Difficult debugging: when an E2E test fails, the failure could be in any of the 10+ systems involved. A unit test failure points directly to the broken function. (4) Maintenance cost: E2E tests require constant updating as the game evolves, consuming QA time that could be spent on exploration and new test development.

rubric.key: Correctly describes the three pyramid layers with relative quantities
Explains slow feedback and brittleness problems of inverted pyramid
Notes debugging difficulty and maintenance cost

questions.question: You are conducting a playtesting session and the player gets stuck on a puzzle for 5 minutes, looking frustrated. Describe the correct facilitator approach and explain why you should not give them the answer.
questions.answer: Continue observing without intervening. Use neutral prompting: 'What are you thinking right now?' or 'What do you think you need to do?' to elicit their thought process. If they are genuinely stuck beyond productive frustration, gently redirect: 'Have you tried interacting with anything else in the room?' Do not give the answer because: (1) The purpose of playtesting is to discover where the design fails to communicate. If you give the solution, you lose the data about why they got stuck. (2) This participant is now contaminated for this puzzle -- you can never test this moment with them again. (3) The frustration itself is data: if multiple players get stuck here, the puzzle needs clearer signposting. (4) Giving help teaches the player that someone will rescue them, changing their behavior for the rest of the session.

rubric.key: Describes non-leading observation with neutral prompts
Explains that the stuck moment is valuable design failure data
Notes participant contamination from giving answers

questions.question: What is pseudo-localization and how does it help find localization bugs before translations are available? Describe the three types of issues it detects.
questions.answer: Pseudo-localization automatically transforms English text into a test version that simulates properties of translated text without needing real translations. 'Start Game' becomes '[Staaart Gaamee !!!]'. Three issues detected: (1) Text truncation: the expanded text (~30% longer) reveals UI containers that cannot accommodate longer translations, showing where German or Finnish text would be cut off. (2) Character encoding: accented characters (a becomes aa with diacritics) reveal places where the rendering pipeline does not support Unicode, displaying garbled text or missing glyphs. (3) Hardcoded strings: any English text that was not extracted into the localization system appears unchanged in the pseudo-localized build, making it immediately visible as untranslatable text that would appear in English in all language versions.

rubric.key: Correctly defines pseudo-localization with transformation example
Identifies text truncation detection from expansion
Identifies encoding issues and hardcoded string detection

questions.question: Name the three most common reasons games fail platform certification on first submission, and for each describe a specific QA test that would catch the issue.
questions.answer: (1) Incorrect button prompts (40% of failures): the game displays Xbox button icons when a PlayStation controller is connected, or shows 'Press A' when using keyboard. QA test: connect each supported input device and verify every on-screen prompt updates to match the active device within 1 second of switching. (2) Save data corruption on power loss (25% of failures): the game save becomes corrupted or lost when the console loses power during a save operation. QA test: initiate a save, forcibly power off the device during the save write, power on, and verify either the save loads correctly or the game falls back to the previous valid save with a user-facing message. (3) Network disconnection crash (20% of failures): the game crashes or hangs when the network connection is lost during online gameplay or server communication. QA test: while connected to an online feature, disconnect the network cable or enable airplane mode and verify the game displays a user-facing error message and returns to a stable state without crashing.

rubric.key: Three common certification failures correctly identified
Specific QA test described for each failure type

questions.question: Describe three game accessibility features from different disability categories and explain why each one also benefits players without disabilities.
questions.answer: (1) Visual - Colorblind mode: replaces red/green color coding with shapes or patterns. Primary benefit: 8% of male players have color vision deficiency. General benefit: also helps in bright sunlight or on poorly calibrated displays where colors wash out. (2) Motor - Remappable controls: allows every game action to be bound to any button. Primary benefit: enables players with limited hand mobility to use adaptive controllers or one-handed setups. General benefit: all players can optimize controls to their preference, especially left-handed players or players who prefer non-standard layouts. (3) Cognitive - Adjustable difficulty: provides easier modes that reduce complexity. Primary benefit: players with cognitive disabilities can engage with the game at their own pace. General benefit: casual gamers, players returning after a break, and anyone who wants to experience the story without challenge frustration.

rubric.key: Three features from different disability categories
Primary accessibility benefit for each
General benefit for non-disabled players for each

quizzes.topic: CI/CD, QA Management, and Release Readiness

questions.question: Describe how a CI pipeline with branch protection prevents buggy code from reaching the main branch. Include the specific steps from developer commit to merge.
questions.answer: Steps: (1) Developer pushes commits to a feature branch and opens a pull request. (2) GitHub Actions CI is triggered: it checks out the branch, builds the Unity project, runs all automated tests (Edit Mode + Play Mode), and optionally runs the performance benchmark. (3) CI reports results as a status check on the pull request (green checkmark for pass, red X for fail). (4) Branch protection rules on the main branch require all CI status checks to pass before merging is allowed. The merge button is disabled until CI passes. (5) If tests fail, the developer reviews the failure logs, fixes the issue in the feature branch, and pushes again, triggering another CI run. (6) Once all checks pass and code review is approved, the merge is allowed. (7) A post-merge CI run on main produces the official build for QA testing. Result: code that breaks automated tests cannot reach the main branch, preventing regressions from propagating to the team.

rubric.key: Correct sequence from PR to CI to merge protection
Explains that merge is blocked until CI passes
Notes the developer fix-and-retry cycle

questions.question: The bug burn-down chart shows that open Critical bugs decreased from 12 to 3 over 4 weeks, but Major bugs increased from 15 to 22 in the same period. The release is in 2 weeks. Assess the situation and make a recommendation.
questions.answer: Assessment: Critical bug trend is positive (12 to 3), indicating the most severe issues are being resolved. However, Major bugs are increasing (15 to 22), meaning new Major bugs are being discovered faster than they are being fixed. This suggests either: (a) testing is uncovering previously unexamined areas, or (b) recent code changes are introducing new issues. If release criteria require < 5 Major bugs and the current trend continues (+7 in 4 weeks, or ~+3.5 in the remaining 2 weeks), the project will have approximately 25 Major bugs at release -- far exceeding the threshold. Recommendation: (1) Triage the 22 Major bugs immediately to identify any that can be reclassified as Minor or deferred. (2) Implement a code freeze except for bug fixes to stop introducing new bugs. (3) Reallocate development resources to Major bug fixing. (4) If the criteria cannot be met in 2 weeks, recommend a 1-2 week delay. The alternative is shipping with known Major issues that damage the game's reception.

rubric.key: Correctly interprets diverging Critical and Major trends
Projects the trend forward and compares to release criteria
Provides actionable recommendation with justification

questions.question: What is defect escape rate and why is it a more meaningful QA effectiveness metric than total bugs found?
questions.answer: Defect escape rate is the percentage of bugs found by players in production that QA should have caught during testing. Calculated as: (production bugs) / (production bugs + bugs found by QA during testing). For example, if QA found 200 bugs and 20 more were found by players after release, the escape rate is 20/220 = 9.1%. It is more meaningful than total bugs found because: (1) Total bugs found is context-dependent -- a buggy game produces many bugs regardless of QA quality. A high bug count might mean QA is excellent (thorough coverage) or the code is terrible. (2) Escape rate directly measures QA's primary mission: preventing bugs from reaching players. A 5% escape rate means 95% of bugs were caught before release. (3) Escape rate enables improvement: tracking it over releases shows whether QA processes are getting better or worse, and analyzing escaped bugs reveals coverage gaps to address.

rubric.key: Correctly defines escape rate with formula
Explains why total bugs found is misleading
Explains how escape rate directly measures QA mission effectiveness

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions and playtesting workshops. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a reduced final grade. Playtesting sessions require advance scheduling with participants and cannot be rescheduled.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement. Bug report portfolios submitted late miss the grading window and receive a maximum of 70%.
course_policies.collaboration: Test plans and bug reports must be individually authored. Students may share game builds for testing but all documentation, analysis, and automation code must be original work. Playtesting partners must be credited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All test code must be original or properly attributed. Copying bug reports from public databases or other students will result in course failure. AI-generated test cases must be disclosed and manually verified for correctness by the student.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have access to a computer capable of running Unity and profiling tools. Lab computers are available with all required software. JIRA accounts will be provided for the course. GitHub accounts are required for CI pipeline assignments.
